words.definition: a proposed explanation that can be tested
example_sentences: The hypothesis was supported by the experimental data.
She formulated a clear hypothesis before beginning the research.
A good hypothesis is specific, measurable, and testable.
words.pronunciation_tip: hy-POTH-ih-sis — four syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'my idea to test' — say 'my hypothesis'.
words.collocations: ['test a hypothesis', 'formulate a hypothesis', 'support a hypothesis']

words.definition: the system of methods used in a particular field of study
example_sentences: The report describes the research methodology in detail.
Different methodologies lead to different results.
The methodology section explains how data was collected.
words.pronunciation_tip: meth-uh-DOL-uh-jee — five syllables, stress on third.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'how I did the research' — say 'my methodology' or 'the research methodology'.

words.definition: a reference to a published work used as evidence
example_sentences: Every claim must be supported by a citation.
The essay included over thirty citations.
She forgot to include a citation for the statistic.
words.pronunciation_tip: sy-TAY-shun — three syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'a reference to another book' — say 'a citation'.

words.definition: a short summary of a research paper at the beginning
example_sentences: Read the abstract before deciding to read the full paper.
Her abstract clearly explained the purpose and findings.
An abstract should be no longer than 250 words.
words.pronunciation_tip: AB-strakt — stress on first syllable when used as a noun.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'the summary of the paper' every time — say 'the abstract'.

words.definition: evaluation of research by other experts in the same field
example_sentences: The article was published after passing peer review.
Peer review ensures quality in academic publishing.
The study was criticised for not being peer reviewed.
words.pronunciation_tip: PEER rih-VYOO.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'checked by other scientists' — say 'peer reviewed'.

words.definition: based on observation and experiment rather than theory
example_sentences: The empirical evidence strongly supports the theory.
Her research is based on empirical data from 500 participants.
Without empirical evidence, a theory remains speculative.
words.pronunciation_tip: em-PIR-ih-kul — four syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'based on real evidence' — say 'empirical evidence' or 'empirical research'.

words.definition: the results or conclusions of a piece of research
example_sentences: The findings suggest a strong link between stress and illness.
Her findings contradicted the previous study.
The report presents the key findings clearly.
words.pronunciation_tip: FYN-dingz — two syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'the results' every time in academic writing — say 'the findings'.

words.definition: important or large enough to have an effect or be noticed
example_sentences: There was a significant improvement in test scores.
The difference between the two groups was statistically significant.
This is a significant development in the field.
words.pronunciation_tip: sig-NIF-ih-kunt — four syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'big' or 'important' in academic writing — say 'significant'.

words.definition: to recognise or admit the existence of something
example_sentences: The paper acknowledges several limitations.
She acknowledged the contribution of her research team.
Good academic writing acknowledges opposing views.
words.pronunciation_tip: ak-NOL-ij — three syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'say that something exists' — say 'acknowledge'.

words.definition: to prove that something is wrong or incorrect
example_sentences: The new data refutes the previous theory.
She refuted the claims made in the earlier study.
It is difficult to refute empirical evidence.
words.pronunciation_tip: rih-FYOOT — two syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'prove wrong' always — say 'refute' in academic contexts.

words.definition: a relationship between two variables that tend to change together
example_sentences: There is a strong correlation between poverty and poor health.
Correlation does not prove causation.
The study found a negative correlation between the two factors.
words.pronunciation_tip: kor-ih-LAY-shun — four syllables, stress on third.
words.common_mistake: Don't confuse correlation (relationship) with causation (cause). Correlation ≠ causation.

words.definition: to express someone else's idea in your own words
example_sentences: She paraphrased the original source rather than quoting directly.
Learn to paraphrase effectively to avoid plagiarism.
It's better to paraphrase than to copy directly.
words.pronunciation_tip: PAIR-uh-frayz — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'I wrote the same thing in different words' — say 'I paraphrased'.

words.definition: the main argument of an essay, or a major piece of original research
example_sentences: State your thesis clearly in the introduction.
She spent three years writing her doctoral thesis.
A strong thesis statement sets up the whole essay.
words.pronunciation_tip: THEE-sis — two syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't confuse thesis (main argument of an essay) with thesis (doctoral research paper) — both are correct uses.

words.definition: the combination of different ideas or sources to create a new understanding
example_sentences: Good academic writing involves synthesis of multiple sources.
The conclusion synthesises the key findings.
Her synthesis of the literature was impressive.
words.pronunciation_tip: SIN-thuh-sis — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'combining ideas' as a noun — say 'synthesis'.

words.definition: relating to the measurement of quantity using numbers
example_sentences: The quantitative data showed a 30% improvement.
She used quantitative methods to analyse the results.
Quantitative research is based on statistics and measurement.
words.pronunciation_tip: KWON-tih-tuh-tiv — five syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't confuse quantitative (numbers-based) with qualitative (qualities-based).

words.definition: logically consistent and clearly expressed
example_sentences: The essay was well-organised and highly coherent.
A coherent argument flows logically from beginning to end.
Her ideas became more coherent as the research developed.
words.pronunciation_tip: koh-HEER-unt — three syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'clear and well-organised' every time — say 'coherent'.

words.definition: a detailed examination and evaluation of something
example_sentences: The essay requires a critical analysis of three texts.
She performed a thorough critical analysis of the policy.
Critical analysis means evaluating strengths and weaknesses.
words.pronunciation_tip: KRIT-ih-kul uh-NAL-ih-sis.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'looking at something carefully' — say 'critical analysis'.
words.collocations: ['perform critical analysis', 'critical analysis of', 'requires critical analysis']

words.definition: a reason offered against a claim or argument
example_sentences: She addressed every counterargument in her thesis.
Acknowledging counterarguments makes your essay stronger.
What is the strongest counterargument to your position?
words.pronunciation_tip: KOWN-ter-ar-gyoo-ment — five syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'the other side's argument' — say 'the counterargument'.

words.definition: to use someone else's work or ideas without acknowledgement
example_sentences: He was caught plagiarising entire paragraphs from another study.
Students who plagiarise face serious academic consequences.
Never plagiarise — always cite your sources.
words.pronunciation_tip: PLAY-juh-ryz — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'he copied' in academic contexts — say 'he plagiarised'.

words.definition: to suggest something without stating it directly
example_sentences: The data implies a link between the two factors.
Are you implying that I'm wrong?
The tone of the report implies serious concern.
words.pronunciation_tip: im-PLY — two syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't confuse imply (speaker suggests) with infer (listener concludes). 'I imply; you infer'.
